frameworks.name: aphantasia as introspective-access deficit
frameworks.one_line: aphantasia impairs introspective access to imagery rather than abolishing imagery itself

frameworks.name: mental imagery during reading
frameworks.one_line: imagery-rich text evokes sensory simulation that shapes gaze on relevant words

frameworks.name: nonconscious imagery account
frameworks.one_line: imagery may persist nonconsciously under aphantasia and still modulate behavior

core_question: Do aphantasics differ from controls in eye-fixation patterns and immersion when reading imagery-rich stories?
key_assumptions: self-reported absence of visual imagery indexes aphantasia
fixation duration on imagery-laden words indexes imagery engagement
perceptual imagery content carries sensory-rich load distinguishable from other text
manipulations: imagery content type within stories (e.g., perceptual vs other imagery)
two short imagery-rich stories read while eye-tracked
key_findings: imagery content influenced gaze fixation similarly in both groups
perceptual imagery content produced the longest fixation durations during reading
aphantasics reported less immersion despite matched fixation patterns
fixation-imagery correlation present in controls but absent or reversed in aphantasics
interpretations: aphantasia may primarily impair introspective access rather than imagery itself
results are consistent with a nonconscious imagery account under aphantasia
limitations_noted: fixation-imagery correlation result described as tentative
